title: Roast Partridge
subtitle: A small, sweet game bird roasted whole at high heat, one per person
excerpt: Partridge is milder and sweeter than pheasant, making it a gentler introduction to game cookery. At 350-400g a bird, one serves one person comfortably. Roast at high heat for 20-25 minutes, barded with bacon, and serve with watercress and game chips.
sourceNotes: Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): partridge roasting notes and accompaniments. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: roasting game birds. Traditional British game cookery.

recipe.temperatureNote: high throughout for the short roast

glossaryTerms.definition: Laying strips of fat bacon across the breast of a lean game bird before roasting to self-baste the meat as the fat renders. Without it, partridge breast will dry in the short time needed for the legs to cook.
The intestines of a game bird, often left inside and eaten on toast as a delicacy when the bird is very fresh. Partridge trail is milder than woodcock or snipe. Butcher-ready birds are usually drawn.

content.text: Partridge is among the most accessible of the British game birds. The flavour is delicate enough that it suits those who find pheasant or grouse too strong, and the cooking time is short: 20-25 minutes at 220°C for a whole bird. The key risk is the same as with pheasant: the breast dries faster than the legs, so
with streaky bacon is standard practice.
Two birds share a roasting pan comfortably. Each weighs 350-400 g, so the cooking time is shorter than for a pheasant; begin checking with a probe at 20 minutes. A
spread on toast was the traditional British accompaniment, though most modern birds arrive drawn. Watercress and a tart jelly such as redcurrant work well regardless.

items.prepNote: partridge, approx. 350-400 g each, one per person, oven-ready

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Pat both birds dry. Rub
over the legs of each bird. Tuck two thyme sprigs inside each cavity. Season the legs with
. Lay the bacon rashers over each breast, covering completely.

Roast for 20 minutes. Remove the bacon and return the birds for a further 5 minutes to colour the breast skin. Probe the thigh at the deepest point; it should read 75°C. Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 10 minutes. The birds are small and will cool quickly, so rest in a warm spot.

to the roasting pan and scrape up the fond over medium heat. Simmer for 3 minutes, season, and strain into a warm jug. Each diner receives one whole bird; carve at the table or serve the birds whole, allowing the diner to pull the legs free.

rows.symptom: Breast is dry even with bacon
rows.cause: Overcooked, or bacon did not fully cover the breast surface
rows.fix: Remove the birds from the oven the moment the probe reads 75°C. Ensure bacon rashers overlap so the breast has no gaps exposed to direct heat.

rows.symptom: Legs are still pink at 25 minutes
rows.cause: Birds were cold from the fridge, or larger than 400 g
rows.fix: Always rest at room temperature for 30 minutes before roasting. Check the weight; a 450 g bird needs 5 extra minutes.

rows.cause: Small birds produce less fond than a large joint
rows.fix: Add a splash of redcurrant jelly or a teaspoon of tomato puree to the pan before adding stock; this deepens the colour and flavour.

rows.symptom: The bird tastes too strong and gamey
rows.cause: Older bird or one hung for longer than 5 days
rows.fix: Buy from a reputable game dealer and specify young birds hung for 3-4 days. Early-season birds (September-October) tend to be milder.

content.text: Partridge is a September-through-February bird in the British calendar, with the grey partridge native to these islands and the red-legged (French) partridge now the commoner shot bird. The roasted whole partridge, served on toast with watercress, has appeared on British dining tables for centuries. It is a simple dish and the right one for a bird this delicate.
